1.1: There was a man in the Land of Hus, named Job, and that man was simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil.
1.2: And there were born to him seven sons and three daughters.
1.3: And his possession was seven thousand sheep, & three thousand camels, also five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she-asses, and a family exceeding great: and that man was great among all them of the East.
1.4: And his sons went, and made a feast by houses, every one in his day. And sending they called their three sisters to eat & drink with them.
1.5: And when the days of feasting had passed about in course, Job sent to them, and sanctified them: and rising up early offered holocausts for every one. For he said: Lest perhaps my sons have sinned, and have blessed God in their hearts. So did Job all the days.
1.6: But on a certain day when the sons of God were come to assist before our Lord, Satan also was present among them.
1.7: To whom our Lord said: From whence comest thou? Who answering, said: I have gone round about the earth, & walked through it.
1.8: And our Lord said to him: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is not the like to him in the earth, a man simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil?
1.9: To whom Satan answering, said: Why, doth Job fear God in vain?
1.10: hast not thou fenced him, and his house, and all his substance round about, blessed the works of his hands, and his possession hath increased on the earth?
1.11: But stretch forth thy hand a little, and touch all things that he possesseth, unless he bless thee in the face.
1.12: Our Lord therefore said to Satan: behold, all things that he hath, are in thy hand, only upon him extend not thy hand. And Satan went forth from the face of our Lord.
1.13: And when upon a certain day his sons and daughters did eat, and drink wine in the house of their eldest brother,
1.14: there came a messenger to Job, which said: The oxen ploughed, and the she-asses fed beside them,
1.15: and the Sabeians came in violently, and have taken all things, and have strucken the servants with the sword, and I only have escaped to tell thee.
1.16: And when he yet spake, another came, and said: The fire of God fell from heaven, and striking the sheep and the servants hath consumed them, and I only have escaped to tell thee.
1.17: But whilst he also was yet speaking, there came another and said: The Chaldees made three troops, and have invaded the camels, and taken them, moreover the servants also they have strucken with the sword, and I alone am fled to tell thee.
1.18: He yet spake, and behold another came in, and said: Thy sons and daughters eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother,
1.19: suddenly a vehement wind came violently from the country of the desert, and shook the four corners of the house, which falling oppressed thy children and they are dead, and I alone have escaped to tell thee.
1.20: Then Job rose up, and rent his garments, & with polled head falling on the ground, adored,
1.21: and said: Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return thither: Our Lord gave, and our Lord hath taken away: as it hath pleased our Lord, so is it done: the name of our Lord be blessed.
1.22: In all these things Job sinned not with his lips, neither spake he any foolish thing against God.

2.1: And it came to pass when on a certain day the sons of God were come, and stood before our Lord, and Satan came among them, and stood in his sight,
2.2: that our Lord said to Satan: From whence comest thou? Who answering said: I have gone round about the earth, and walked through it.
2.3: And our Lord said to Satan: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is not the like to him in the earth, a man simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil, and yet retaining innocency? But thou hast moved me against him, that I should afflict him in vain.
2.4: To whom Satan answering, said: Skin for skin, and all things that a man hath, he will give for his life:
2.5: otherwise put thy hand, and touch his bone and flesh, and then shalt thou see that he will bless thee in the face.
2.6: Our Lord therefore said to Satan: Behold he is in thy hand, but yet save his life.
2.7: Satan therefore going out from the face of our Lord, struck Job with a very sore boil, from the sole of the foot even to the top of his head:
2.8: who with a shell scraped the corruption, sitting on a dunghill.
2.9: And his wife said to him: Doest thou yet continue in thy simplicity? Bless God and die.
2.10: Who said to her: Thou hast spoken like one of the foolish women: if we have received good things of the hand of God, evil things why should we not receive? In all these things Job sinned not with his lips.
2.11: Therefore Job's three friends hearing all the evil, that had chanced to him, came every one out of their place, Eliphaz a Themanite, and Baldad a Suhite, and Sophar a Naamathite. For they had appointed, that coming together they would visit him, and comfort him.
2.12: And when they had lifted up their eyes a far off, they knew him not, and crying out they wept, and renting their cloths sprinkled dust over their head toward heaven.
2.13: And they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no man spake to him a word: for they saw the pain to be vehement.

3.1: After these things Job opened his mouth, and cursed his day,
3.3: Perish may the day wherein I was born, & the night wherein it was said: A man is conceived.
3.4: Be that day turned into darkness, God require it not from above, and let it not be lightened with light.
3.5: Let darkness, and the shadow of death obscure it, let a mist possess it, and be it wrapped in bitterness.
3.6: Adarksome hurl wind possess that night, be it not counted in the days of the year, nor numbered in the months.
3.7: Be that night solitary, nor praise-worthy.
3.8: Let them curse it which curse the day, which are ready to raise up Leviathan.
3.9: Let the stars be darkened with the mist thereof: let it expect light and let it not see, neither the rising of the appearing morning.
3.10: Because it shut not up the doors of the womb that bare me, nor took away evils from mine eyes.
3.11: Why died I not in the matrice, perished not forthwith being come forth of the womb?
3.12: Why received upon the knees: why nursed with the breasts?
3.13: For now sleeping I should be quiet, and should rest in my sleep:
3.14: With kings and consuls of the earth, which build themselves solitary places:
3.15: Or with Princes, that posses gold, and replenish their houses with silver:
3.16: Or as a thing untimely born that is hid I should not be, or as they that being conceived have not seen the light.
3.17: There the impious have ceased from tumult, & there the wearied with strength have rested.
3.18: And they sometime bound together without grief, have not heard the voice of the exactor.
3.19: Little and great are there, and the servant free from his master.
3.20: Why is there light given to a miserable man, and life to them, that are in bitterness of soul,
3.21: that expect death, and it cometh not, as they that dig up treasure,
3.22: and they rejoice exceedingly when they have found the grave?
3.23: To a man whose life is hid, and God hath compassed him with darkness?
3.24: Before I eat I sigh: and as it were overflowing waters, so my roaring:
3.25: Because the fear which I feared, hath chanced to me: and that which I was afraid of, hath happened.
3.26: Have I not dissembled? Have I not kept silence? Have I not been at ease? And indignation is come upon me.

4.1: But Eliphaz the Themanite answering, said:
4.2: If we shall begin to speak to thee, perhaps thou wilt take it grievously, but the word conceived who can hold?
4.3: Behold thou hast taught many, and weary hands thou hast strengthened.
4.4: Them that wavered thy words have confirmed, and trembling knees thou hast strengthened:
4.5: But now a plague is come upon thee, & thou hast fainted: hath touched thee, and thou art troubled.
4.6: Where is thy fear, thy strength, thy patience, and the perfection of thy ways?
4.7: Remember I beseech thee, who ever being innocent hath perished? Or when have the just been destroyed?
4.8: Yea rather I have seen them that work iniquity, and sow sorrows, and reap them,
4.9: to have perished by the blast of God, and with the spirit of his wrath to have been consumed.
4.10: The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the lioness, and the teeth of the whelps of lions are bruised:
4.11: The tiger hath perished, because he had no prey, and the lions' whelps are destroyed.
4.12: Moreover to me there was spoken a secret word, and as it were by stealth hath mine ear received the veins of the whispering thereof.
4.13: In the horror of a vision by night, when deep sleep is wont to hold men,
4.14: fear held me, and trembling, and all my bones were made sore afraid:
4.15: And when the spirit passed in my presence, the hairs of my flesh stood upright.
4.16: There stood one, whose countenance I knew not, an image before mine eyes, and I heard the voice as it were of a gentle wind:
4.17: What, shall man be justified in comparison of God, or shall a man be more pure than his maker?
4.18: Behold they that serve him are not stable, and in his Angels he found wickedness:
4.19: How much more they that inhabit houses of clay, which have an earthly foundation, shall be consumed as it were of the moth?
4.20: From morning until evening they shall be cut down: and because none understandeth, they shall perish for ever.
4.21: And they that shall be left, shall be taken away from them: they shall die, and not in wisdom.

5.1: Call therefore if there be that will answer thee, and turn to some of the Saints.
5.2: Anger indeed killeth the foolish, and envy slayeth the little one.
5.3: I have seen a fool with firm root, and I cursed his beauty by and by.
5.4: His children shall be made far from salvation, and shall be destroyed in the gate, and there shall be none to deliver.
5.5: Whose harvest the hungry shall eat, and the armed shall take him by violence, and the thirsty shall drink his riches.
5.6: Nothing in the earth is done without a cause, and out of the ground sorrow shall not rise.
5.7: Man is born to labour, and the bird to flight.
5.8: For the which thing I will beseech our Lord, and toward God I will set my speech:
5.9: Who doeth great and unsearchable and marvelous things without number:
5.10: Who giveth rain upon the face of the earth, and watereth all things with waters:
5.11: Who setteth the humble on high, and them that are in heaviness he comforteth with health:
5.12: Who dissipateth the cogitations of the malignant, that their hands cannot accomplish that which they began:
5.13: Who apprehendeth the wise in their subtilty, and dissipateth the counsel of the wicked:
5.14: By day they shall incur darkness, and as it were in the night, so shall they grope at noon days.
5.15: Moreover he shall save the needy from the sword of their mouth, and the poor from the hand of the violent.
5.16: And to the needy there shall be hope, but iniquity shall draw together her mouth.
5.17: Blessed is the man that is corrected of God: refuse not therefore the chastising of our Lord:
5.18: Because he woundeth, and cureth: striketh, & his hands shall heal.
5.19: In six tribulations he shall deliver thee, and in the seventh evil shall not touch thee.
5.20: In famine he shall deliver thee from death, and in battle, from the hand of the sword.
5.21: From the scourge of the tongue thou shalt be hid; and thou shalt not fear calamity when it cometh.
5.22: In waste and famine thou shalt laugh, & the beasts of the earth thou shalt not fear.
5.23: But with the stones of the lands thy covenant, and the beasts of the earth shall be peaceable to thee.
5.24: And thou shalt know that thy tabernacle hath peace, and visiting thy beauty, thou shalt not sin.
5.25: Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be manifold, and thy progeny as the grass of the earth.
5.26: Thou shalt enter into the grave in aboundance, as a heap of wheat is carried in his time.
5.27: Behold, this is even so, as we have searched out: which thou having heard revolve in thy mind.

6.2: would God my sins were weighed, whereby I have deserved wrath, and the calamity, which I suffer, in a balance.
6.3: As the sand of the sea this would appear heavier, wherefore my words also are full of sorrow:
6.4: Because the arrows of our Lord be in me, the indignation whereof drinketh up my spirit, & the terrors of our Lord war against me.
6.5: Will the wild ass roar when he hath grass? Or the oxe loweth when he shall stand before the full manger?
6.6: Or can an unsavoury thing be eaten, that is not seasoned with salt? Or can a man taste that which being tasted bringeth death?
6.7: The things which before my soul would not touch, now for anguish are my meats.
6.8: Who will grant that my petition may come: and that God would give me that which I expect?
6.9: And he that hath begun, the same would loose his hand, and cut me off?
6.10: And this might be my comfort, that afflicting me with sorrow, he spare not, nor I gainsay the words of the Holy one.
6.11: For what is my strength, that I can sustain it? Or what is mine end, that I should do patiently?
6.12: Neither is my strength the strength of stones, neither is my flesh of brass.
6.13: Behold there is no help for me in myself, and my familiar friends also are departed from me.
6.14: He that taketh away mercy from his friend, forsaketh the fear of our Lord.
6.15: My brethren have passed by me, as the torrent that passeth swiftly in the valleys.
6.16: They that fear the hoarfrost, snow shall fall upon them.
6.17: At the time, when they shall be dissipated they shall perish: & after they wax hot they shall be dissolved out of their place.
6.18: The paths of their steps are entangled: they shall walk in vain, & shall perish.
6.19: Consider ye the paths of Thema, the ways of Saba, and expect a little while.
6.20: They are confounded, because I have hoped: they are come also even unto me, and are covered with shame.
6.21: Now you are come: and even now seeing my plague you are afraid.
6.22: Have I said: Bring ye to me, and of your substance give to me?
6.23: Or deliver me from the hand of the enemy, and out of the hand of the strong deliver me?
6.24: Teach ye me, and I will hold my peace: and if I perhaps have been ignorant in any thing, instruct ye me.
6.25: Why have you detracted from the words of truth, whereas there is none of you that can control me?
6.26: To rebuke only you frame speeches, and you utter words in the wind.
6.27: You rush in upon a pupil, and you endeavour to overthrow your friend.
6.28: Notwithstanding accomplish that which you have begun: give ear, and see whether I lie.
6.29: Answer I beseech you without contention: and speaking that which is just, judge ye.
6.30: And you shall not find iniquity in my tongue, neither shall folly sound in my jaws.

7.1: The life of man upon earth is a warfare, and his days, as the days of an hired man.
7.2: As a servant desireth the shadow, and as the hired man tarrieth for the end of his work:
7.3: so I also have had vain months, & have numbered to myself laborious nights.
7.4: If I sleep, I shall say: When shall I arise? & again I shall expect the evening, & shall be replenished with sorrows even until darkness.
7.5: My flesh is clothed with rottenness & filth of dust, my skin is withered, & drawn together.
7.6: My days have passed more swiftly, than the web is cut of the weaver, and are consumed without any hope.
7.7: Remember that my life is a wind, & mine eye shall not return to see good things.
7.8: Neither shall the sight of man behold me: thine eyes upon me, and I shall not stand.
7.9: As a cloud is consumed, and passeth away: so he that shall descend to hell shall not ascend.
7.10: Neither shall he return any more into his house, neither shall his place know him any more.
7.11: Wherefore I also will not spare my mouth, I will speak in the tribulation of my spirit: I will talk with the bitterness of my soul.
7.12: Why, am I a sea, or a whale, that thou hast compassed me with a prison?
7.13: If I say: My little bed shall comfort me, and I shall be relieved speaking with myself in my couch:
7.14: Thou wilt terrify me by dreams, and by visions shake me with horror.
7.15: For the which thing my soul hath chosen hanging, and my bones death.
7.16: I have despaired. I shall now live no longer: Spare me, for my days are nothing.
7.17: What is man that thou magnifiest him? Or why settest thou thy heart toward him?
7.18: Thou doest visit him early, and suddenly thou provest him:
7.19: How long doest thou not spare me, nor suffer me that I swallow my spittle?
7.20: I have sinned, what shall I do to thee, o keeper of men? Why hast thou set me contrary to thee, and I am become burdenous to myself?
7.21: Why doest thou not take away my sin, and why doest thou not take away mine iniquity? Behold now I shall sleep in the dust: and if thou seek me in the morning, I shall not be.

8.1: But Baldad the Suhite answering, said:
8.2: How long wilt thou speak such things, & shall the spirit of the word of thy mouth be multiplied?
8.3: Why, doth God supplant judgment? Or doth the Omnipotent subvert that which is just?
8.4: Although thy children have sinned to him, and he hath left them in the hand of their iniquity:
8.5: Yet if thou wilt arise early to God, & wilt beseech the Omnipotent:
8.6: If thou wilt walk clean & upright, he will forthwith awake unto thee, and will make the habitation of thy justice peaceable:
8.7: In so much, that if thy former things have been little, thy later things may be multiplied exceedingly.
8.8: For ask the old generation, and search diligently the memory of the fathers.
8.9: (For we are but as yesterday, & are ignorant that our days upon the earth are as a shadow.)
8.10: And they shall teach thee: they shall speak to thee, and from their heart shall utter words.
8.11: Can the rush be green without moisture? Or a sedgy place grow without water?
8.12: When it is yet in his flower, and is not plucked with the hand, it withereth before all herbs:
8.13: Even so the ways of all, that forget God, and the hope of the hypocrite shall perish:
8.14: His folly shall not please him, and his confidence as the spiders' web.
8.15: He shall lean upon his house, and it shall not stand: he shall stay it up, and it shall not rise:
8.16: He seemeth moistened before the sun come, & in his rising his blossom shall go forth.
8.17: Upon a heap of rocks his roots shall be thick, and among stones he shall abide.
8.18: If he swallow him up out of his place, he will deny him, and will say: I know thee not.
8.19: For this is the joy of his way, that others may spring again of the earth.
8.20: God will not reject the simple, nor reach his hand to the malignant.
8.21: Until thy mouth be filled with laughter, & thy lips with jubilation.
8.22: They that hate thee, shall be clothed with confusion: and the tabernacle of the impious shall not stand.

9.2: Indeed I know it is so, and that man is not justified compared with God.
9.3: If he will content with him, he cannot answer him one for a thousand.
9.4: He is wise of heart, and strong of force: who hath resisted him, and hath had peace?
9.5: He that transported mountains, and they whom he subverted in his fury, knew not.
9.6: He that removeth the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof are shaken.
9.7: He that commandeth the sun, and it riseth not: and shutteth up the stars as it were under a seal:
9.8: He that alone spreadeth the heaven, & goeth upon the waves of the sea.
9.9: He that maketh Arcturus, and Orion, and Hyades, and the inner parts of the south.
9.10: He that doth great things and incomprehensible, and marvelous, of the which there is no number.
9.11: If he come to me, I shall not see him: if he depart, I shall not understand.
9.12: If suddenly he ask, who shall answer him? Or who can say: Why doest thou so?
9.13: God whose wrath no man can resist, and under whom they stoop that carry the world.
9.14: How great am I then, that I may answer him, and speak in my words with him?
9.15: Who although I have any just thing, will not answer, but will beseech my judge.
9.16: And when he shall hear me invocating, I do not believe that he hath heard my voice.
9.17: For in a hurlwind shall he break me, and shall multiply my wounds yea without cause.
9.18: He granteth not my spirit to rest, and he filleth me with bitterness.
9.19: If strength be demanded, he is most strong: if equity of judgment, no man dare give testimony for me.
9.20: If I will justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I will shew myself innocent, he shall prove me wicked.
9.21: Although I shall be simple, the self-same shall my soul be ignorant of, and I shall be weary of my life,
9.22: One thing there is that I have spoken, both the innocent and the impious he consumeth.
9.23: If he scourge, let him kill at once, and not laugh at the pains of innocents.
9.24: The earth is given into the hands of the impious, he covereth the face of the judges thereof: and if it be not he, who is it then?
9.25: My days have been swifter than a post: they have fled and have not seen good.
9.26: They have passed by as ships carrying fruits, as an eagle flying to meat.
9.27: When I shall say: I will not speak so, I change my face, & am tormented with sorrow.
9.28: I feared all my works, knowing that thou didst not spare the offender.
9.29: But if so also I am impious, why have I laboured in vain?
9.30: If I be washed as it were with snow-waters, & my hands shall shine as most clean:
9.31: Yet shall thou dip me in filth, and my garments shall abhor me.
9.32: For neither I will answer a man that is like myself: nor that may be heard with me equally in judgment.
9.33: There is none that may be able to reprove both, and to put his hand between both.
9.34: Let him take his rod from me, and let not his dread terrify me.
9.35: I will speak, and will not fear him: for I cannot answer fearing.

10.1: My soul is weary of my life, I will let my speech pass against myself. I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
10.2: I will say to God: Condemn me not: tell me why thou judgest me so.
10.3: Doth it seem good to thee, if thou calumniate me, and oppress me the work of thy hands, and help the counsel of the impious?
10.4: Hast thou eyes of flesh: or as a man seeth, shalt thou also see?
10.5: Are thy days as the days of man, and are thy years as the times of men:
10.6: That thou seekest my iniquity, and searchest my sin?
10.7: And thou mayst know that I have done no impious thing, whereas there is no man that can deliver out of thy hand.
10.8: Thy hands have made me, & framed me wholly round-about, & dost thou so suddenly cast me down headlong?
10.9: Remember I beseech thee that as clay thou madest me, & into dust thou wilt bring me again.
10.10: Hast thou not as milk milked me, and curded me as cheese?
10.11: With skin & flesh thou hast clothed me: with bones and sinews thou hast compacted me.
10.12: Life and mercy thou hast given to me, and thy visitation hath kept my spirit.
10.13: Although thou conceal these things in thy heart, yet I know that thou rememberest all things.
10.14: If I have sinned and thou hast spared me for an hour: why doest thou not suffer me to be clean from mine iniquity?
10.15: And if I shall be impious, woe is to me: and if just, I shall not lift up my head, filled with affliction and misery.
10.16: And for pride as a lioness thou wilt take me, and returning thou doest marvelously torment me.
10.17: Thou renewest thy witnesses against me, & multipliest thy wrath toward me, and pains do war upon me.
10.18: Why didst thou bring me forth out of the matrice? Who would God, I had been consumed, that eye might not see me.
10.19: I had been as if I were not, carried from the womb to the grave.
10.20: Shall not the fewness of my days be ended shortly? suffer me therefore, that I may a little lament my sorrow:
10.21: Before I go, and return not, unto the dark land, that is covered with the mist of death:
10.22: Aland of misery and darkness, where is the shadow of death, and no order, but everlasting horror inhabiteth.

11.1: But Sophar the Naamathite answering, said:
11.2: Why, shall he that speaketh many things, not hear also? Or shall a man full of words be justified?
11.3: To thee only shall men hold their peace? & when thou hast mocked others, shalt thou be confuted of none?
11.4: For thou hast said: My word is pure, and I am clean in thy sight.
11.5: And I would wish that God would speak with thee, and would open his lips to thee.
11.6: That he might shew thee the secrets of wisdom, and that his law is manifold, and thou mightest understand that thou art exacted much lesser things of him than thy iniquity deserveth.
11.7: Peradventure thou wilt comprehend the steps of God, and wilt find out the Omnipotent perfectly?
11.8: He is higher than heaven, and what wilt thou do? Deeper than hell, and how wilt thou know?
11.9: The measure of him is longer than the earth, & broader than the sea.
11.10: If he shall overthrow all things, or shall straiten them into one, who shall say against him?
11.11: For he knoweth the vanity of men, & seeing iniquity doth he not consider?
11.12: Avain man is extolled into pride, & thinketh himself born free as a wild ass's colt.
11.13: But thou hast confirmed thy heart, & hast spread thy hands to him.
11.14: If thou shalt take away from thee the iniquity that is in thy hand, & injustice remain not in thy tabernacle:
11.15: Then mayst thou lift up thy face without spot, & thou shalt be stable, & shalt not fear.
11.16: Thou shalt also forget misery, & shalt remember it as waters that are passed.
11.17: And the brightness as it were of noon days, shall arise to thee at evening: and when thou shalt think thyself consumed, thou shalt rise as the day star.
11.18: And thou shalt have confidence, hope being set before thee, & buried thou shalt sleep secure.
11.19: Thou shalt rest, and there shall be none to terrify thee: and very many shall beseech thy face.
11.20: But the eyes of the impious shall decay, and escape shall fail them, and their hope the abomination of the soul.

12.2: Are you then men alone, & shall wisdom die with you?
12.3: I also have a heart even as you, neither am I inferior to you: for who is ignorant of these things, which you know?
12.4: He that is mocked of his friend as I, shall invocate God, and he will hear him: for the simplicity of the just man is scorned.
12.5: The lamp contemned in the cogitations of the rich, is prepared to the time appointed.
12.6: The tabernacles of robbers abound, and they provoke God boldly, whereas he hath given all things into their hands.
12.7: For ask the beasts, and they shall teach thee: and the fowls of the air, and they shall tell thee.
12.8: Speak to the earth, and it shall answer thee, and the fishes of the sea shall tell.
12.9: Who is ignorant that the hand of our Lord hath made all these things?
12.10: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the spirit of all the flesh of man.
12.11: Doth not the ear discern words, and the jaws of him that eateth, the taste?
12.12: In the ancients is wisdom, and in long time prudence.
12.13: With him is wisdom and strength, he hath counsel and understanding.
12.14: If he shall destroy, there is no man that can build: if he shut up a man, there is none that can open.
12.15: If he hold in the waters, all things shall be dried: and if he send them forth, they shall overthrow the earth.
12.16: With him is strength and wisdom: he knoweth both the deceiver, and him that is deceived.
12.17: He bringeth counsellers to a foolish end, & judges to astonishment.
12.18: He looseth the belt of kings, & girdeth their reins with a cord.
12.19: He leadeth away Priests without glory, & supplanteth the great men.
12.20: Changing the lip of the true, and taking away the doctrine of the ancients.
12.21: He poureth out contempt upon Princes, relieving them that had been oppressed.
12.22: Who revealeth profound things out of darkness, and bringeth forth the shadow of death into light.
12.23: Who multiplieth nations, & destroyeth them, and restoreth the destroyed whole again.
12.24: Who changeth the heart of the Princes of the people of the earth, and deceiveth them, that they may go in vain where is no passage.
12.25: They shall grope as in the dark, and not in the light, and he shall make them go amiss as drunken men.

13.1: Behold mine eye hath seen all these things, and mine ear hath heard, and I have understood every thing.
13.2: According to your knowledge I also do know: neither am I inferior to you.
13.3: But yet I will speak to the Omnipotent, and I covet to dispute with God.
13.4: First shewing you to be forgers of lying, and maintainers of perverse opinions.
13.5: And would God ye would hold your peace, that you might be thought to be wise men.
13.6: Hear ye therefore my correptions, and attend the judgment of my lips.
13.7: Hath God need of your lie, that for him you speak guiles?
13.8: Do you take his person, and do you endeavour to judge for God?
13.9: Or shall it please him from whom nothing can be concealed, or shall he be deceived as a man, with your fraudulent dealings?
13.10: He shall reprove you, because in secret you take his person.
13.11: Forthwith as he shall move himself, he shall trouble you: and his terror shall come violently upon you.
13.12: Your memory shall be compared to ashes, and your necks shall be brought into clay.
13.13: Hold your peace a little while, that I may speak whatsoever my mind shall prompt me.
13.14: Why do I tear my flesh with my teeth, and carry my soul in my hands?
13.15: Although he shall kill me, I will trust in him: but yet I will reprove my ways in his sight.
13.16: And he shall be my saviour: for no hypocrite shall come in his sight.
13.17: Hear ye my word, and receive the obscure sayings with your ears.
13.18: If I shall be judged, I know that I shall be found just.
13.19: What is he that will be judged with me? Let him come: why am I consumed holding my peace?
13.20: Two things only do not to me, and then shall I not be hid from thy face:
13.21: Make thy hand far from me, and let not thy fear terrify me.
13.22: Call me, and I will answer thee: or else I will speak, and do thou answer me.
13.23: How great iniquities and sins I have, my wicked deeds and my offences shew thou me.
13.24: Why hidest thou thy face, and thinkest me thine enemy?
13.25: Against the leaf, that is violently taken with the wind, thou shewest thy might, and persecutest dry stubble.
13.26: For thou writest bitterness against me, and wilt consume me with the sins of my youth.
13.27: Thou hast put my feet in the stocks, and hast observed all my paths, and hast considered the steps of my feet.
13.28: Who as rottenness am to be consumed, and as a garment, that is eaten of the moth.

14.1: Man born of woman, living a short time, is replenished with many miseries.
14.2: Who as a flower cometh forth and is destroyed, and flyeth as a shadow, and never abideth in the same state.
14.3: And doest thou count it a worthy thing to open thine eyes upon such an one, and to bring him with thee into judgment?
14.4: Who can make clean him that is conceived of unclean seed? Is it not thou which only art?
14.5: The days of man are short, and the number of his months is with thee, thou hast appointed his limits which cannot be passed.
14.6: Depart a little from him, that he may rest, until his day wished for come, even as the hired man's.
14.7: Atree hath hope: if it be cut, it waxeth green again, and the boughs thereof spring.
14.8: If his root be old in the earth, and the trunk thereof be dead in the dust,
14.9: At the scent of water it shall spring, and bring forth leaves, as when it was first planted.
14.10: But when man shall be dead, and naked, and consumed, where is he I pray?
14.11: As if the waters should depart out of the sea, and a river made empty should be dried up.
14.12: So man when he is asleep shall not rise again, till heaven perish, he shall not awake, nor rise up out of his sleep.
14.13: Who will grant me this, that in hell thou protect me, and hide me till thy fury pass, and appoint me a time, wherein thou wilt remember me?
14.14: Shall man that is dead, thinkest thou, live again? All the days, in which I am now in warfare, I expect until my change do come.
14.15: Thou shalt call me, and I shall answer thee: to the work of thy hands thou shalt reach thy right hand.
14.16: Thou indeed hast numbered my steps: but thou wilt spare my sins.
14.17: Thou hast sealed my offences as it were in a bag, but hast cured mine iniquity.
14.18: Amountain falling slideth down, and a rock is removed out of his place.
14.19: Waters make stones hollow, and with inundation the earth by little and little is consumed: and men therefore thou shalt destroy in like manner.
14.20: Thou hast strengthened him a little, that he might pass away for ever: thou shalt change his face, and shalt send him forth.
14.21: Whether his children shall be noble, or unnoble, he shall not understand.
14.22: But yet his flesh whilst he shall live shall have sorrow, and his soul shall mourn upon himself.

15.1: But Eliphaz the Themanite answering, said:
15.2: Will a wise man answer as it were speaking into the wind, and fill his stomach with burning?
15.3: Thou reprovest him in words, that is not equal to thee, & speakest that which is not expedient for thee.
15.4: As much as is in thee, thou hast evacuated fear, and hast taken away prayers before God.
15.5: For thine iniquity hath taught thy mouth, and thou doest imitate the tongue of blasphemers.
15.6: Thine own mouth shall condemn thee, and not I: and thy lips shall answer thee.
15.7: Wast thou the first man born, and formed before the little hills?
15.8: Hast thou heard God's counsel, and shall his wisdom be inferior to thee?
15.9: What doest thou know, that we are ignorant of? What doest thou understand that we know not?
15.10: There are both old men & ancients among us, much elder than thy fathers.
15.11: Is it a great matter that God should comfort thee? But thy naughty words hinder it.
15.12: Why doth thy heart elevate thee, & as thinking great things, hast thou astonished eyes?
15.13: Why doeth thy spirit swell against God, to utter such words out of thy mouth?
15.14: What is man, that he should be without spot, & that the born of a woman should appear just?
15.15: Behold among his saints none is immutable, & the heavens are not clean in his sight.
15.16: How much more is man abominable, and unprofitable, who drinketh iniquity as it were water?
15.17: I will shew thee, hear me: that which I have seen I will tell thee.
15.18: Wise men confess and hide not their fathers:
15.19: to whom only the earth was given, and stranger hath not passed by them.
15.20: The impious is proud all his days, and the number of the years of his tyranny is uncertain.
15.21: The sound of terror is always in his ears: and when there is peace, he always suspecteth treason.
15.22: He believeth not that he may return from darkness to light, looking round about for the sword on every side.
15.23: When he shall move himself to seek bread, he knoweth that the day of darkness is prepared in his hand.
15.24: Tribulation shall terrify him, and distress shall compass him, as a king that is prepared to battle.
15.25: For he hath stretched his hand against God, & is strengthened against the Omnipotent.
15.26: He hath run against him with neck set upright, and is armed with a fat neck.
15.27: Fatness hath covered his face, and from his sides there hangeth tallow.
15.28: He hath dwelt in desolate cities, and in desert houses, that are brought into hillocks.
15.29: He shall not be enriched, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he put his root in the earth.
15.30: He shall not depart out of darkness, the flame shall dry his boughs, and he shall be taken away with the spirit of his own mouth.
15.31: He shall not believe vainly deceived with error, that he may be redeemed with any price.
15.32: Before his days be accomplished, he shall perish: and his hands shall wither.
15.33: His cluster in the first flower shall be hurt as a vine, and as the olive tree casting his flower.
15.34: For the congregation of the hypocrite is barren, and fire shall devour their tabernacles, which gladly take gifts.
15.35: He conceived sorrow, and hath brought forth iniquity, and his womb prepareth guiles.

16.2: I have heard oftentimes such things, heavy comforters you are all.
16.3: Shall words full of wind have an end? Or is any thing troublesome to thee, if thou speak?
16.4: I also could speak things like to you: and would God your soul were for my soul.
16.5: I also would comfort you with words, and would wag my head upon you.
16.6: I would strengthen you with my mouth, and would move my lips, as sparing you.
16.7: But what shall I do? If I speak, my pain will not rest: and if I hold my peace, it will not depart from me.
16.8: But now my sorrow hath oppressed me, & all my limbs are brought to nothing.
16.9: My wrinkles give testimony against me, and a false speaker is raised up against my face contradicting me.
16.10: He hath gathered his fury upon me, and threatening me hath gnashed against me with his teeth, mine enemy hath beheld me with terrible eyes.
16.11: They have opened their mouths upon me, and exprobating have strucken my cheek, they are filled with my pains.
16.12: God hath shut me up with the wicked man, and hath delivered me to the hands of the impious.
16.13: Isometime that wealthy-one suddenly am broken: he hath held my neck, broken me, and set me to himself as it were a mark.
16.14: He hath compassed me with his spears, he hath wounded my loins, he hath not spared, and hath poured out on the earth my bowels.
16.15: He hath cut me with wound upon wound, he hath come violently upon me as it were a giant.
16.16: I have sowed sackcloth upon my skin, and have covered my flesh with ashes.
16.17: My face is swollen with weeping, and my eye-lids are dim.
16.18: These things have I suffered without the iniquity of my hand, whereas I had clean prayers to God.
16.19: Earth cover not my blood, neither let my cry find place in thee to be hid.
16.20: For behold my witness is in heaven, and he that knoweth my conscience on high.
16.21: My friends full of words: mine eye distilleth unto God.
16.22: And would God a man might so be judged with God, as the son of man is judged with his companion.
16.23: For behold the short years pass away, and I walk the path, by the which I shall not return.

17.1: My spirit shall be extenuated, my days shall be shortened, and the grave only remaineth for me.
17.2: I have not sinned, and mine eye abideth in bitterness.
17.3: Deliver me, and set me beside thee, and let any man's hand fight against me.
17.4: Thou hast made their heart far from discipline, and therefore they shall not be exalted.
17.5: He promiseth a prey to his fellows, and the eyes of his children shall fail.
17.6: He hath set me as it were for a proverb of the common people, and I am an example before them.
17.7: Mine eye is dim for indignation, and my members are brought as it were to nothing.
17.8: The just shall be astonished upon this, and the innocent shall be raised up against the hypocrite.
17.9: And the just shall hold his way, & with clean hands shall add strength.
17.10: Be all you therefore converted, and come, and I shall not find among you any wise man.
17.11: My days have passed, my cogitations are dissipated, tormenting my heart:
17.12: Night they have turned into day, and again after darkness I hope for light.
17.13: If I shall expect, hell is my house, and in darkness I have made my bed.
17.14: I have said to rottenness: Thou art my father, my mother, and my sister, to worms.
17.15: Where is now then my expectation, and my patience who considereth.
17.16: All my things shall descend into most deep hell: there at the least, shall I have rest thinkest thou?

18.1: But Baldad the Suhite answering, said:
18.2: How long will ye speak vaunting words? Understand ye first, and so let us speak.
18.3: Why are we reputed as beasts, and accounted vile before you?
18.4: Which destroyest thy soul in thy fury, shall the earth be forsaken for thee, and shall rocks be transported out of their place?
18.5: Shall not the light of the impious be extinguished, and the flame of his fire not shine?
18.6: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and the candle that is over him, shall be extinguished.
18.7: The steps of his power shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down headlong.
18.8: For he hath thrust his feet into a net, and walketh in the mashes thereof.
18.9: The sole of his foot shall be held in a snare, and thirst shall burn against him.
18.10: His gin is hid in the earth, and his spring upon the path.
18.11: Fears shall terrify him on every side, and his feet shall entangle him.
18.12: Let his strength be extenuated with famine, and let hunger invade his ribs.
18.13: Let it devour the beauty of his skin, let death the first-born consume his arms.
18.14: Let his confidence be plucked away out of his tabernacle, and let destruction as a king tread upon him.
18.15: Let the companions of him, that is not, dwell in his tabernacle, let brimstone be sprinkled in his tent.
18.16: Let his roots be dried downward, and his harvest destroyed upward.
18.17: Let the memory of him perish from the earth, and let not his name be renowned in the streets.
18.18: He shall expel him out of light into darkness, and shall transport him out of the world.
18.19: His seed shall not be, nor his progeny in his people, nor any remnants in his countries.
18.20: In his day the last shall be astonished, and horror shall invade the first.
18.21: These are then the tabernacles of the wicked man, and this is the place of him that knoweth not God.

19.2: How long do you afflict my soul, and wear me with words?
19.3: Behold, ten times you confound me, and are not ashamed oppressing me.
19.4: For although I have been ignorant, mine ignorance shall be with me.
19.5: But you are set up against me, & reprove me with reproaches.
19.6: At the least now understand ye, that God hath afflicted me not with equal judgment, and hath compassed me with his scourges.
19.7: Behold I shall cry suffering violence, and no man will hear: I shall cry out, and there is none to judge.
19.8: He hath hedged my path round about and I cannot pass, and in my way hath put darkness.
19.9: He hath spoiled me of my glory, and hath taken the crown from my head.
19.10: He hath destroyed me on every side, and I perish, and as it were from a tree plucked hath he taken away my hope.
19.11: His fury is wrath against me, and he hath so accounted me as his enemy.
19.12: His servants have come together, and have made themselves a way by me, and have besieged my tabernacle round about.
19.13: He hath made my brethren far from me, and my acquaintance as strangers have departed from me.
19.14: My kinsmen have forsaken me, and they that knew me have forgotten me.
19.15: The guests of my house, and my maidservants have counted me an alien, and I have been as it were a stranger in their eyes.
19.16: I called my servant, and he did not answer me, with mine own mouth I besought him.
19.17: My wife hath abhorred my breath, and I prayed the children of my womb.
19.18: Fools also despised me, and when I was departed from them, they backbited me.
19.19: My counsellers sometime have abhorred me: & he whom I loved most hath turned against me.
19.20: The flesh being consumed, my bone hath cleaved to my skin, and there are left only lips about my teeth.
19.21: Have mercy upon me, have mercy upon me, at the least you my friends, because the hand of our Lord hath touched me.
19.22: Why do you as God persecute me, and are filled with my flesh?
19.23: Who will grant me that my words may be written? Who will give me that they may be drawn in a Book,
19.24: with iron pen, & in plate of lead, or else with stile might be graven in flint-stone?
19.25: For I know that my Redeemer liveth, & in the last day I shall rise out of the earth.
19.26: And I shall be compassed again with my skin, and in my flesh I shall see God.
19.27: Whom I myself shall see, & mine eyes shall behold, & none other: this my hope is laid up in my bosom.
19.28: Why then do you say now: Let us persecute him, & let us find root of word against him?
19.29: Fly therefore from the face of the sword, because the sword is the revenger of iniquities: and know ye that there is judgment.

20.1: But Sophar the Naamathite answering, said:
20.2: Therefore my diverse cogitations succeed one another, and my mind is rapt into sundry things.
20.3: The doctrine, wherewith thou reprovest me, I will hear, and the spirit of my understanding shall answer me.
20.4: This I know from the beginning, since man was placed upon the earth,
20.5: That the praise of the impious is short, and the joy of the hypocrite as it were for a moment:
20.6: If his pride rise up even to the heaven, and his head touch the clouds:
20.7: As a dunghill in the end he shall be destroyed, and they that had seen him, shall say: Where is he?
20.8: As a dream that flyeth away he shall not be found, he shall pass as a vision by night:
20.9: The eye that had seen him, shall not see him, neither shall his place behold him any more.
20.10: His children shall come to naught with poverty, and his hands shall render him his sorrow.
20.11: His bones shall be filled with the vices of his youth, & they shall sleep with him in the dust.
20.12: For when evil shall be sweet in his mouth, he will hide it under his tongue.
20.13: He will spare it, & not leave it, and will hide it in his throat.
20.14: His bread in his belly shall be turned into the gall of asps within him.
20.15: The riches which he hath devoured, he shall vomit out, & God shall draw them forth out of his belly.
20.16: He shall suck the head of asps, and the viper's tongue shall kill him.
20.17: (Let him not see the streams of the river, the torrents of honey, & of butter.)
20.18: He shall be punished for all things that he did, & yet not be consumed: according to the multitude of his inventions so also shall he suffer.
20.19: Because breaking in he hath made the poor naked: he hath violently taken a house, & built it not.
20.20: Neither is his belly filled: and when he shall have the things he coveted, he cannot possess them.
20.21: There remained not of his meat, & therefore nothing shall continue of his goods:
20.22: When he shall be filled, he shall be straitened, he shall burn, and all sorrow shall fall upon him.
20.23: Would God his belly were filled, that he may send forth the wrath of his fury upon him, and rain his battle upon him.
20.24: He shall fly weapons of iron, and shall fall upon a bow of brass.
20.25: The sword plucked out, and coming forth of his scabbard, and glistering in his bitterness: the horrible shall go and come upon him.
20.26: All darkness is hid in his secrets: fire that is not kindled shall devour him, he shall be afflicted left in his tabernacle.
20.27: The heavens shall reveal his iniquity, and the earth shall rise against him.
20.28: The blossom of his house shall be opened, he shall be plucked down in the day of God's fury.
20.29: This is the portion of an impious man from God, & the inheritance of his words from our Lord.

21.2: Hear I beseech you my words, and do penance.
21.3: Bear with me, that I also may speak, and after my words, if it shall seem good, laugh ye.
21.4: Is my disputation against man, that I ought not worthily to be sorry?
21.5: Harken to me, and be astonished, and put the finger upon your mouth.
21.6: And I when I shall remember, am afraid, and trembling shaketh my flesh.
21.7: Why then do the impious live, are they advanced, & strengthened with riches?
21.8: Their seed continueth before them, a multitude of kinsmen, & of nephews in their sight.
21.9: Their houses be secure & peaceable, & the rod of God is not upon them.
21.10: Their bullock hath conceived, & hath not made abortion: their cow hath calved, and is not deprived of her calf.
21.11: Their little ones go forth as flocks, and their infants rejoice with pastimes.
21.12: They hold the timbrel, & the harp, & rejoice at the sound of the organ.
21.13: They lead their days in wealth, and in a moment they go down to hell.
21.14: Who said to God: depart from us, we will not the knowledge of thy ways.
21.15: Who is the Omnipotent, that we should serve him? And what doth it profit us if we shall pray him?
21.16: But yet because their good things are not in their hand, be the counsel of the impious far from me.
21.17: How often shall the candle of the impious be extinguished, and inundation come upon them, and shall he divide the sorrows of his fury?
21.18: They shall be as chaff before the face of the wind, and as ashes, which the whirlwind scattereth.
21.19: God shall reserve the sorrow of the father to his children: and when he shall have rendered it, then shall he know.
21.20: His eyes shall see his own slaughter, and he shall drink the fury of the Omnipotent.
21.21: For what doeth it pertain to him concerning his house after him: although the number of his months be diminished the half?
21.22: Shall any man teach God knowledge, who judgeth the high ones?
21.23: This man dieth strong and in health, rich and happy.
21.24: His bowels be full of fat, and his bones be embrued with marrow.
21.25: But another dieth in bitterness of soul without any riches:
21.26: And yet they shall sleep together in the dust, and worms shall cover them.
21.27: Surely I know your cogitations, & unjust sentences against me.
21.28: For you say: Where is the house of the Prince? & where are the tabernacles of the impious?
21.29: Ask any of the wayfaring men, & you shall understand that he knoweth these self-same things.
21.30: Because the evil man is kept unto the day of perdition, and he shall be led to the day of fury.
21.31: Who shall reprove his way before him? And who shall repay him the things that he hath done?
21.32: He shall be brought to the graves, and shall watch in the head of the dead.
21.33: He hath been sweet to the gravel of Cocytus, and after him he shall draw every man, and before him innumerable.
21.34: How therefore do ye comfort me in vain, whereas your answer is shewed to be repugnant to the truth?

22.1: But Eliphaz the Themanite answering, said:
22.2: Can man be compared with God, yea though he be of perfect knowledge?
22.3: What doth it profit God if thou be just? Or what doest thou advantage him if thy way be unspotted?
22.4: Shall he be afraid to reprove thee, and come with thee into judgment:
22.5: And not for thy very great malice, and thine infinite iniquities?
22.6: For thou hast taken away the pledge of thy brethren without cause, and the naked thou hast spoiled of cloths.
22.7: Water to the weary thou hast not given, and from the hungry thou hast withdrawn bread.
22.8: In the strength of thine arm thou didst possess the earth, and being the mightiest thou didst obtain it.
22.9: Widows thou hast sent away empty, and the arms of pupils thou hast broken in pieces.
22.10: Therefore art thou compassed with snares, and sudden fear troubleth thee.
22.11: And thoughtest thou that thou shouldest not see darkness, and that thou shouldest not be oppressed with the violence of overflowing waters?
22.12: Doest thou not think that God is higher than heaven, and is exalted above the top of the stars?
22.13: And thou sayst: For what knoweth God? And he judgeth as it were by a mist.
22.14: The clouds are his covert, neither doth he consider our things, and he walketh about the poles of heaven.
22.15: Doest thou covet to keep the path of worlds, which wicked men have trodden?
22.16: Who were taken away before their time, and a flood hath overthrown their foundation.
22.17: Who said to God: Depart from us; and as though the Omnipotent could do nothing, they esteemed him:
22.18: Whereas he had filled their houses with good things, whose sentence be far from me.
22.19: The just shall see, & shall rejoice, & the innocent man shall scorn them.
22.20: Is not their exaltation cut down, and hath not fire devoured the remnants of them?
22.21: Agree thou therefore to him, and have peace: and by these things thou shalt have the best fruits.
22.22: Receive the law of his mouth, and put his words in thy heart.
22.23: If thou wilt return to the Omnipotent, thou shalt be builded up, and shalt make iniquity far from thy tabernacle.
22.24: He shall give for earth flint, and for flint torrents of gold.
22.25: And the Omnipotent shall be against thine enemies, and silver shall be heaped together unto thee.
22.26: Then shalt thou abound in delights upon the Omnipotent, and shalt lift up thy face to God.
22.27: Thou shalt ask him, and he will hear thee, and thou shalt pay thy vows.
22.28: Thou shalt decree a thing, and it shall come to thee, and light shall shine in thy ways.
22.29: For who will be humbled, shall be in glory: & he that will bow down his eyes, he shall be saved.
22.30: The innocent shall be saved, but shall be saved in the cleanness of his hands.

23.2: Now also my talk is in bitterness, and the hand of my plague is aggravated upon my mourning.
23.3: Who will grant me that I may know and find him, & come even to his throne?
23.4: I will set judgement before him, and will fill my mouth with accusations.
23.5: That I may know the words that he will answer me, and understand what he will speak to me.
23.6: I will not that he contend with me with much strength, nor that he oppress me with the weight of his greatness.
23.7: Let him propose equity against me, and my judgment shall come to victory.
23.8: If I shall go to the East, he appeareth not: if to the West, I shall not understand him.
23.9: If to the left hand, what shall I do? I shall not apprehend him: if I turn myself to the right hand, I shall not see him.
23.10: But he knoweth my way, and hath proved me as gold that passeth through the fire:
23.11: My foot hath followed his steps, I have kept his way, & have not declined out of it.
23.12: From the commandments of his lips I have not departed, and I have hid the words of his mouth in my bosom.
23.13: For he is alone, and no man can turn away his cogitation: and whatsoever his soul would, that hath he done.
23.14: And when he shall have fulfilled his will in me, many other things also are at hand with him.
23.15: And therefore I am troubled at his face, and considering him I am made pensive with fear.
23.16: God hath mollified my heart, and the Omnipotent hath troubled me.
23.17: For I have not perished because of the imminent darkness, neither hath the mist covered my face.

24.1: Times are not hid from the Omnipotent: but they that know him, know not his days.
24.2: Some have transferred bounds, spoiled flocks, & fed them.
24.3: They have driven away the ass of pupils, and have taken away the widow's oxe for a pledge.
24.4: They have subverted the way of the poor, and have oppressed together the meek of the earth.
24.5: Others as wild asses in the desert go forth to their work: watching to the prey, do prepare bread for their children.
24.6: They reap the field that is not theirs, and gather the grapes of his vineyard, whom by violence they have oppressed.
24.7: They send men away naked taking away their cloths, which have no covering in the cold.
24.8: Whom the showers of the mountains do wash, & not having a covert, they embrace stones.
24.9: They did violence spoiling the pupils, & the common poor people they spoiled.
24.10: From the naked & them that go without clothing, and the hungry they have taken away the ears of corn.
24.11: They have rested the noontide among their heaps, which having trodden the wine presses are a thirst.
24.12: Out of the cities they have made men to mourn, and the soul of the wounded hath cried, & God doth not suffer it to pass unrevenged:
24.13: They have been rebellious to the light, they have not known his ways, neither did they return by his paths.
24.14: At the very break of day the murderer riseth, he killeth the needy, and the poor man: but by night he will be as a thief.
24.15: The eye of the adulterer observeth darkness, saying: Eye shall not see me: and he will cover his face.
24.16: He diggeth through houses in the dark, as in the day they had appointed with themselves, and they have not known the light.
24.17: If suddenly the morning shall appear, they think it the shadow of death: and they walk so in darkness as it were in light.
24.18: He is light above the face of the water: cursed be his portion in the earth, neither walk he by the way of the vineyards.
24.19: Let him pass from snow waters to exceeding heat, and his sin even unto hell.
24.20: Let mercy forget him: worms his sweetness: be he not in remembrance, but be he broken in pieces as an unfruitful tree.
24.21: For he hath fed the barren, and her that beareth not, and to the widow he hath not done good.
24.22: He hath pulled down the strong in his strength and when he shall stand, he will not credit his life.
24.23: God hath given him place for penance, and he abuseth it unto pride: but his eyes be upon his ways.
24.24: They are elevated for a little, and shall not stand, and shall be humbled as all things, and shall be taken away, and as the tops of the ears of corn they shall be broken.
24.25: And if it be not so, who can reprove me that I have lied, and set my words before God?

25.1: But Baldad the Suhite answering, said:
25.2: Power and terror is with him, that maketh concord in his high ones.
25.3: Is there any number of his soldiers? And upon whom shall not his light arise?
25.4: Can man be justified compared with God, or the born of a woman appear clean?
25.5: Behold the moon also doth not shine, and the stars are not clean in his sight.
25.6: How much more man rottenness, and the son of man a worm?

26.2: Whose helper are thou? His that is weak? And doest thou hold up the arm of him, that is not strong?
26.3: To whom hast thou given counsel? Perhaps to him, that hath not wisdom, and thy prudence hast thou shewed very great.
26.4: Whom wouldest thou teach? Not him that made breath?
26.5: Behold the giants groan under the waters, & they that dwell with them.
26.6: Hell is naked before him, & there is no covert to perdition.
26.7: Who stretcheth out the north-wind over the vacant, and hangeth the earth upon nothing.
26.8: Who bindeth the waters in his clouds, that they break not forth together downward.
26.9: Who holdeth the countenance of his throne, & spreadeth his cloud over it.
26.10: He hath made a limit about the waters, till light and darkness be ended.
26.11: The pillars of heaven tremble, and dread at his beck.
26.12: In his strength suddenly the seas are gathered together, and with his wisdom he struck the proud man.
26.13: His spirit hath adorned the heavens & his hand being the mid-wife, the winding serpent is brought forth.
26.14: Lo, these things are said in part of his ways: and where as we have heard scarce a little drop of his word, who shall be able to behold the thunder of his greatness?

27.1: Job also added, taking his parable, and said:
27.2: God liveth, who hath taken away my judgment, and the Omnipotent, which hath brought my soul to bitterness.
27.3: That as long as breath remaineth in me, and the spirit of God in my nostrils,
27.4: My lips shall not speak iniquity, neither shall my tongue meditate lying.
27.5: God forbid that I should judge you to be just: till I fail, I will not depart from mine innocency.
27.6: My justification which I have begun to hold, I will not forsake: for my heart doth not reprehend me in all my life.
27.7: Let mine enemy be as the impious, and mine adversary as the wicked one.
27.8: For what is the hypocrite's hope, if covetously he take by violence, and God deliver not his soul?
27.9: Will God hear his cry, when distress shall come upon him?
27.10: Or can he be delighted in the Omnipotent, and invocate God at all time?
27.11: I will teach you by the hand of God, what the Omnipotent hath, neither will I hide it.
27.12: Lo, you do all know, and why speak you vain things without cause?
27.13: This is the portion of an impious man with God, and inheritance of the violent, which they shall receive of the Omnipotent.
27.14: If his children be multiplied, they shall be in the sword, & his nephews shall not be filled with bread.
27.15: They that shall be left of him, shall be buried in death, and his widows shall not weep.
27.16: If he shall heap together silver as earth, and as clay shall prepare garments:
27.17: He shall prepare indeed, but the just man shall be clothed with them: and the innocent shall divide the silver.
27.18: He hath built his house as a moth, and as a keeper hath he made a bower.
27.19: The rich man when he shall sleep shall take away nothing with him: he shall open his eyes, and find nothing.
27.20: Poverty as water shall apprehend him, in the night a tempest shall oppress him.
27.21: The burning wind shall take him up, and carry him away, and as a whirlwind shall pull him violently out of his place.
27.22: And he shall cast upon him, and shall not spare: out of his hand flying he shall fly.
27.23: He shall clasp his hands upon him, and shall hiss upon him, beholding his place.

28.1: Silver hath beginnings of her veins, and gold hath a place, wherein it is molten.
28.2: Iron is taken out of the earth, & stone resolved with heat, is turned into brass.
28.3: He hath set a time for darkness, & the end of all things he considereth, the stone also of darkness, and the shadow of death.
28.4: The torrent divideth from the pilgrim people, them whom the foot of the needy man hath forgotten, and to whom there is no way.
28.5: The land, out of which bread grew in his place, is destroyed with fire.
28.6: The place of the sapphire the stones thereof, and the clods of it gold.
28.7: The bird hath not known the path, neither hath the eye of the vulture beheld it.
28.8: The children of merchants have not trodden it, neither hath the lioness passed by it.
28.9: He hath stretched forth his hand to the flint, he hath overthrown mountains from the roots.
28.10: In the rocks he hath cut out rivers, and his eye hath seen every precious thing.
28.11: The depths also of rivers he hath searched, & hid things he hath brought forth to light:
28.12: But where is wisdom to be found, and what is the place of understanding?
28.13: Man knoweth not the price thereof, neither is it found in the land of them that live pleasantly.
28.14: The depth saith: It is not in me: and the sea speaketh: It is not with me.
28.15: The finest gold shall not be given for it, neither shall silver be weighed in the change thereof.
28.16: It shall not be compared with the dyed colours of India, nor with Sardonyx the most precious stone, or with the Sapphire.
28.17: Gold or glass shall not be equal to it, neither shall vessels of gold be changed for it.
28.18: High and eminent things shall not be mentioned in comparison of it: and wisdom is drawn out of secret places.
28.19: The topazius of Æthiopia shall not be equal to it, neither shall it be compared to the cleanest dying.
28.20: From whence then cometh wisdom? And what is the place of understanding?
28.21: It is hid from the eyes of all living, the fowls of the air also know it not.
28.22: Perdition & death have said: With our ears we have heard the fame thereof.
28.23: God understandeth the way of it, & he knoweth the place thereof.
28.24: For he beholdeth the ends of the world, & looketh on all things that are under heaven.
28.25: Who made a poise to the winds, & weighed the waters in a measure.
28.26: When he gave a law to the rains, & a way to the sounding storms.
28.27: Then he saw it, and declared, and prepared, and searched it.
28.28: And he said to man: Behold the fear of our Lord, that is wisdom: and to depart from evil, understanding.

29.1: Job also added, taking his parable, and said:
29.2: Who will grant me, that I may be according to the former months, according to the days in which God kept me?
29.3: When his lamp shined over my head, & I walked by his light in darkness?
29.4: As I was in the days of my youth, when God was secretly in my tabernacle?
29.5: When the Omnipotent was with me: and my servants round about me?
29.6: When I washed my feet with butter, and the rock poured the rivers of oil?
29.7: When I went forth to the gate of the city, and in the street they prepared me a chair?
29.8: Young men saw me, and hid themselves: and old men rising up stood.
29.9: The Princes ceased to speak, and did put the finger upon their mouth.
29.10: Dukes held in their voice, and their tongue cleaved to their throat.
29.11: The ear hearing counted me blessed, and the eye seeing gave testimony to me:
29.12: For that I had delivered the poor man crying out, and the pupil, that had no helper.
29.13: The blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me, and I comforted the heart of the widow.
29.14: I was clothed with justice: and I revested me with my judgment, as with a garment and crown.
29.15: I was an eye to the blind, and a foot to the lame.
29.16: I was the father of the poor: and the cause which I knew not, I searched most diligently.
29.17: I brake the jaws of the wicked man, and out of his teeth I took away the prey.
29.18: And I said: I will die in my little nest, and as a palmtree will multiply days.
29.19: My root is opened beside the waters, and dew shall continue in my harvest.
29.20: My glory shall always be renewed, and my bow in my hand shall be repaired.
29.21: They that heard me, expected sentence, and attent held their peace at my counsel.
29.22: To my words they durst add nothing, and my speech distilled upon them.
29.23: They expected me as rain, and they opened their mouth as it were to a lateward shower.
29.24: If at any time I laughed on them, they believed not, and the light of my countenance fell not on the earth.
29.25: If I would have gone to them, I sat first, and when I sat as a king with his army standing about him, yet was I a comforter of them that mourned.

30.1: But now they of younger time scorn me, whose fathers I vouchsafed not to put with the dogs of my flock:
30.2: The force of whose hands was to me as nothing, & they were thought unworthy of life it-self.
30.3: Barren with poverty and famine, who gnawed in the wilderness, ill favoured by calamity and misery.
30.4: And they did eat grass, and the barks of trees, and the root of junipers was their meat.
30.5: Who taking these things violently out of the valleys, when they had found every thing, they ran to them with a cry.
30.6: They dwelt in the deserts of torrents, and in caves of the earth, or upon gravel.
30.7: Who rejoiced among these kind of things, and counted it delicacies to be under the briars.
30.8: The children of foolish and base men, and in the earth not appearing at all.
30.9: Now am I turned into their song, and become a proverb with them.
30.10: They abhor me, and fly far from me, and are not afraid to spit in my face.
30.11: For he hath opened his quiver, and hath afflicted me, and hath put a bridle into my mouth.
30.12: At the right hand of me rising, my calamities forthwith arose: they have overthrown my feet, & as with waves have oppressed with their paths.
30.13: They have dissipated my ways, they have lien in wait against me, and they have prevailed, and there was not that would help.
30.14: As when a wall is broken, and the gate opened, they have broken violently upon me, and are come tumbling down to my miseries.
30.15: I am brought to nothing: as a wind thou hast taken away my desire: and my prosperity hath passed away as a cloud.
30.16: And now my soul withereth in myself, and the days of affliction possess me.
30.17: In the night my bone is pierced with sorrows: and they that eat me, sleep not.
30.18: In the multitude of them my garment is consumed, and they have girded me about, as it were with the collar of a coat.
30.19: I am compared to durt, and am resembled to imbers and ashes.
30.20: I cry to thee, and thou hearest me not: I stand, and thou doest not respect me.
30.21: Thou art changed to be cruel toward me, and in the hardness of thy hand thou art against me.
30.22: Thou didst lift me up, and setting me as it were upon the wind thou hast mightily dashed me.
30.23: I know that thou wilt deliver me to death, where a house is appointed for every one that liveth.
30.24: But yet not to my consumption doest thou send forth thy hand: and if they shall fall, thou wilt save.
30.25: I wept sometime upon him that was afflicted, & my soul had compassion on the poor.
30.26: I expected good things, and evils are come upon me: tarried for light, and darkness brake forth.
30.27: My inner parts have boiled without any rest, the days of affliction have prevented me.
30.28: I went mourning without fury, rising up, I cried in the multitude.
30.29: I was the brother of dragons, and fellow of Ostriches:
30.30: My skin is made black upon me, and my bones are dried with heat.
30.31: My harp is turned into mourning, and my instrument into the voice of weepers.

31.1: I have made a covenant with mine eyes, that I would not so much as think of a virgin.
31.2: For what part should God from above have in me, and inheritance the Omnipotent from on high?
31.3: Is there not perdition to the wicked man, and alienation to them that work injustice?
31.4: Doth not he consider my ways, and number all my steps?
31.5: If I have walked in vanity, and my foot hath hastened in guile:
31.6: Let him weigh me in a just balance, and let God know my simplicity.
31.7: If my step have declined out of the way, and if my heart hath followed mine eyes, and if spot hath cleaved to my hands:
31.8: Let me sow, and let another eat it: and let my progeny be plucked up by the roots.
31.9: If my heart hath been deceived upon a woman, and if I have lien in wait at my friend's door:
31.10: Let my wife be the harlot of another man, and let other men lie with her.
31.11: For this is a heinous thing, and most great iniquity.
31.12: It is a fire devouring even to perdition, and rooting up all things that spring.
31.13: If I have contemned to abide judgment with my man-servant, & my maid-servant, when they had any controversy against me.
31.14: For what shall I do when God shall rise to judge? And when he shall ask, what shall I answer him?
31.15: Did not he make me in the womb that made him also: & did not one form me in the matrice?
31.16: If I have denied to the poor, that which they would, & have made the eyes of the widow to expect:
31.17: If I have eaten my morsel alone, and the pupil hath not eaten thereof with me.
31.18: (Because from mine infancy mercy hath grown with me: and from my mother's womb it came forth with me.)
31.19: If I have despised him that perisheth, for that he had not clothing, and the poor man without wherewithal to cover him:
31.20: If his sides have not blessed me, and he was not warmed with the fleeces of my sheep:
31.21: If I have lifted up my hand over the pupil, yea when I saw myself in the gate the superior:
31.22: Let my shoulder fall from his juncture, and let my arm with his bones be broken.
31.23: For I have always feared God as waves swelling upon me, & his weight I could not bear.
31.24: If I have thought gold my strength, and have said to fine gold: My confidence.
31.25: If I have rejoiced upon my great riches, and because my hand found many things.
31.26: If I saw the sun when it shined, and the moon going clearly:
31.27: And my heart in secret rejoiced, and I kissed my hand with my mouth.
31.28: Which is most great iniquity, and a denial against God the most high?
31.29: If I have been glad of his fall that hated me, and have rejoiced that evil had found him.
31.30: For I have not given my throat to sin, that cursing I wished his soul.
31.31: If the men of my tabernacle have not said: Who will give of his flesh that we may be filled?
31.32: The stranger tarried not without, my door was open to the wayfaring man.
31.33: If as man I have hid my sin, and have concealed my iniquity in my bosom.
31.34: If I have been afraid at a very great multitude, and the contempt of kinsmen hath terrified me: and I have not rather held my peace, & not gone out of the door.
31.35: Who will grant me an hearer, that the Omnipotent would hear my desire: and that himself that judgeth would write a book.
31.36: That I may carry it on my shoulder, and put it about me as a crown?
31.37: At every step of mine I will pronounce it, and as to the Prince I will offer it.
31.38: If my land cry against me, & with it the furrows thereof lament:
31.39: If I have eaten the fruits thereof without money, and have afflicted the soul of the tillers thereof.
31.40: For wheat let the briar grow to me, and for barley the thorn.

32.1: But these three men omitted to answer Job, for that he seemed just to himself.
32.2: And Eliu the son of Barachel a Buzite, of the kindred of Ram, was angry and took indignation: and he was angry against Job, for that he said himself to be just before God.
32.3: Moreover against his friends he had indignation, for that they had not found a reasonable answer, but only had condemned Job.
32.4: Therefore Eliu expected Job speaking, because they were his elders that spake.
32.5: But when he saw that the three were not able to answer, he was wrath exceedingly.
32.6: And Eliu the son of Barachel a Buzite answering, said: I am younger in time, and you more ancient, therefore casting down my head, I was afraid to shew you my sentence.
32.7: For I hoped that longer age would speak, and that a multitude of years would teach wisdom.
32.8: But as I see, there is a Spirit in men, and the inspiration of the Omnipotent giveth understanding.
32.9: They of many years are not the wise men, neither do the ancients understand judgment.
32.10: Therefore will I speak: Hear ye me, I also will shew you my wisdom.
32.11: For I have expected your words, I have heard your wisdom, as long as you contended in words.
32.12: And as long as I thought you said somewhat, I considered: but as I see, here is none of you that can reprove Job, and answer to his words.
32.13: Lest perhaps you may say: We have found wisdom, God hath rejected him, not man.
32.14: He hath spoken nothing to me, and I will not answer him according to your words.
32.15: They were afraid, and answered no more, and they have taken away talk from themselves.
32.16: Therefore because I have expected, and they spake not: they stood, and answered no more:
32.17: I also will answer my part, & will shew my knowledge.
32.18: For I am full of words, and the spirit of my belly straiteneth me.
32.19: Behold, my belly is as new wine without a vent, which breaketh new vessels.
32.20: I will speak, and take breath a little: I will open my lips, and will answer.
32.21: I will not accept the person of a man, & I will not make God equal to man.
32.22: For I know not how long I shall continue, and whether after a while my maker will take me away.

33.1: Hear therefore Job my sayings, and harken to all my words.
33.2: Behold I have opened my mouth, let my tongue speak within my jaws.
33.3: My words are of my simple heart, and my lips shall speak a pure sentence.
33.4: The Spirit of God made me, and the breadth of the Omnipotent gave me life.
33.5: If thou canst, answer me, and stand against my face.
33.6: Behold God hath made me also even as thee, and of the same clay I also was formed.
33.7: But yet let not my miracle terrify thee, and let not my eloquence be burdenous to thee.
33.8: Thou therefore hast said in my ears, and I have heard the voice of thy words:
33.9: I am clean, and without sin: unspotted, and there is no iniquity in me.
33.10: Because he hath found quarrels in me, therefore hath he thought me his enemy.
33.11: He hath put my feet in the stocks, he hath observed all my ways.
33.12: This therefore is it, wherein thou art not justified: I will answer thee, that God is greater than man.
33.13: Doest thou contend against him because he hath not answered thee to all words?
33.14: God, speaketh once, and repeateth not the self-same the second time.
33.15: By a dream in a vision by night, when heavy sleep falleth upon men, and they sleep in their bed.
33.16: Then doth he open the ears of men, and teaching instructeth them with discipline.
33.17: That he may turn a man from these things, which he doth, and may deliver him from pride:
33.18: Delivering his soul from corruption: and his life, that it pass not unto the sword.
33.19: He rebuketh also by sorrow in the bed, and he maketh all his bones to wither.
33.20: Bread is become abominable to him in his life, and to his soul the meat before desired.
33.21: His flesh shall consume, and the bones that had been covered, shall be made naked.
33.22: His soul hath approached to corruption, and his life to things causing death.
33.23: If there shall be an Angel speaking for him, one of thousands, to declare man's equity.
33.24: He shall have mercy on him, and shall say: deliver him, that he descend not into corruption: I have found wherein I may be propitious to him.
33.25: His flesh is consumed with punishments, let it return to the days of his youth.
33.26: He shall beseech God, and he will be pacified towards him: and he shall see his face in jubilation, and he will render to a man his justice.
33.27: He shall behold men, and shall say: I have sinned, and indeed I have offended, and as I was worthy, I have not received.
33.28: He hath delivered his soul that it should not go into death, but living should see the light.
33.29: Behold all these things doth God work three times in every one.
33.30: That he may reclaim their souls from corruption, and illuminate them with the light of the living.
33.31: Attend Job, and hear me: and hold thy peace, whilst I speak.
33.32: But if thou hast what to speak, answer me, speak, for I would thee to appear just.
33.33: And if thou have not, hear me: hold thy peace, & I will teach thee wisdom.

34.1: Eliu therefore pronouncing spake these words also:
34.2: Hear ye wise men my words: and ye learned harken to me:
34.3: For the ear proveth words, and the throat discerneth meats by the taste.
34.4: Let us choose us judgment, and among us let us see what is the better.
34.5: Because Job said: I am just, and God hath subverted my judgment.
34.6: For in judging me there is a lie: mine arrow is violent without any sin.
34.7: What man is there as is Job, that drinketh scorning as it were water?
34.8: What goeth with them that work iniquity, and walketh with impious men?
34.9: For he hath said: Man shall not please God although he run with him.
34.10: Therefore ye discreet men hear me, far from God be impiety, and iniquity from the Omnipotent.
34.11: For he will render a man his work, & according to the ways of every one he will recompense them.
34.12: For in very deed God will not condemn without cause, neither will the Omnipotent subvert judgment.
34.13: What other hath he appointed over the earth? or whom hath he set over the world, which he made?
34.14: If he direct his heart to him, he shall draw his spirit and breath unto him.
34.15: All flesh shall fail together, and man shall return into ashes.
34.16: If then thou have understanding, hear that is said, and harken to the voice of my speech.
34.17: Can he that loveth not judgment, be healed? And how doest thou so far condemn him that is just?
34.18: That saith to the king, Apostata: that calleth dukes impious:
34.19: Who accepteth not the persons of Princes: nor hath known the tyrant, when he contended against the poor man: for all are the work of his hands.
